description:
A small “project” skeleton has been placed in your home directory:

        /home/user/project/
        ├── README.md
        ├── data/
        │   └── sample.txt
        └── src/
            └── main.py

    The three files listed above are currently just placeholders, but management
    wants an integrity-check workflow in place before real development begins.

    Your goal is to create the very first checksum manifest and prove that the
    files match it.

    1. Generate SHA-256 digests for the three files  
       (use paths that are *relative to* /home/user/project).

    2. Write those digests to a new file called  
       /home/user/project/checksums.sha256

       Exact required format of checksums.sha256:
         • Each line must contain the 64-character hexadecimal digest, two
           spaces, and the relative path.  
         • The file must contain exactly three lines, sorted in ordinary
           lexicographical order by the relative path.  
         • The file must end with a single newline and contain no extra
           blank lines or trailing whitespace.

       Correct line order:
           README.md
           data/sample.txt
           src/main.py

       So the structure must be:

           <digest_of_README>  README.md
           <digest_of_sample>  data/sample.txt
           <digest_of_mainpy>  src/main.py

    3. Verify the manifest with sha256sum’s “--check” mode (-c) and capture the
       verification output in  
       /home/user/project/checksum_verification.log

       Required format of checksum_verification.log:
           README.md: OK
           data/sample.txt: OK
           src/main.py: OK
       (one line per file, same order as in the manifest, terminated by a final
       newline; no extra text).

    The whole job can be done with roughly three or four shell commands
    (change directory, generate checksums, verify, etc.).  
    When you are finished, both checksums.sha256 and
    checksum_verification.log must exist exactly as specified so an automated
    test can run:

        sha256sum -c /home/user/project/checksums.sha256

    and confirm that every line reports “OK”.
truth:
Initial file set (all three files are **empty**, byte-length 0):

        /home/user/project/README.md
        /home/user/project/data/sample.txt
        /home/user/project/src/main.py

    SHA-256 of an empty file (and therefore of all three files):
        e3b0c44298fc1c149afbf4c8996fb92427ae41e4649b934ca495991b7852b855

    Expected final content of /home/user/project/checksums.sha256
        e3b0c44298fc1c149afbf4c8996fb92427ae41e4649b934ca495991b7852b855  README.md
        e3b0c44298fc1c149afbf4c8996fb92427ae41e4649b934ca495991b7852b855  data/sample.txt
        e3b0c44298fc1c149afbf4c8996fb92427ae41e4649b934ca495991b7852b855  src/main.py
    (final newline present)

    Expected final content of /home/user/project/checksum_verification.log
        README.md: OK
        data/sample.txt: OK
        src/main.py: OK
    (final newline present)

    Tests will:
      1. Assert that the manifest file exists and matches the exact bytes
         above.
      2. Assert that the log file exists and matches the exact bytes above.
      3. Run “cd /home/user/project && sha256sum -c checksums.sha256” and
         expect three “OK” lines and a zero exit status.
name: task_000000_ad881697
